mnemonics: Norm is a ruler for vectors: ||v|| measures the 'length' just like a physical ruler. Orthogonal means 'right-angled' in vector space, just like perpendicular axes on a graph.
Use the Pythagorean Theorem: Two vectors are orthogonal if and only if ||u + v||² = ||u||² + ||v||². It's the familiar a² + b² = c² from geometry.
keyPoints: The length (or norm) of a vector v in an inner product space is defined as ||v|| = √⟨v, v⟩.
Two vectors u and v are orthogonal if their inner product is zero: ⟨u, v⟩ = 0.
The Pythagorean Theorem holds in inner product spaces: If u and v are orthogonal, then ||u + v||² = ||u||² + ||v||².
commonMistakes: Forgetting to take the square root when calculating the norm, i.e., writing ||v|| = ⟨v, v⟩ instead of ||v|| = √⟨v, v⟩.
Assuming that if ⟨u, v⟩ = 0, then either u or v must be the zero vector. Non-zero vectors can also be orthogonal.
